title: Chemical Bonding and Shape of Molecules
subtitle: Chapter 4: Chemical Bonding — Complete NEB Notes

content.text: 1. Introduction to Chemical Bonding

Chemical bond is the attractive force that holds various constituents (atoms, ions, or molecules) together in a chemical species. Bond formation results in decrease in energy and increase in stability.
Valence Electrons: Electrons in the outermost shell of an atom are called valence electrons. Only valence electrons participate in chemical bonding.
2. Kossel-Lewis Approach to Chemical Bonding
In 1916, Kossel and Lewis independently proposed a theory to explain chemical bonding based on the inertness of noble gases.
Lewis Symbols: Dots around the element symbol represent valence electrons. Example: Oxygen (O) with 6 valence electrons is shown as :O: with 6 dots.
2.1 Octet Rule
Atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve a stable configuration of 8 electrons in their valence shell (like noble gases). This is called the octet rule.
Exceptions to Octet Rule: • Hydrogen and Helium (need only 2 electrons) • Elements beyond period 2 can have expanded octet (PCl₅, SF) • Molecules with odd electrons (NO, NO₂)

content.label: 💡 Quick Tip
content.text: Noble gases (except He) have 8 valence electrons → very stable → chemically inert. All other elements try to achieve this configuration.
3. Lewis Structures
Lewis structure is a diagrammatic representation showing chemical bonds and lone pairs of electrons in a molecule.
Examples: • H₂O: H-O-H with 2 lone pairs on O • NH₃: N with 3 H atoms and 1 lone pair • CH₄: C with 4 H atoms (tetrahedral) • CO₂: O=C=O (linear) • N₂: N≡N (triple bond)

content.label: ✏️ Drawing Steps
content.text: 1. Count total valence electrons • 2. Write skeletal structure • 3. Place single bonds • 4. Complete octets • 5. Check formal charges
4. Types of Chemical Bonds
There are three main types of chemical bonds: Ionic (Electrovalent), Covalent, and Metallic bonds.
4.1 Ionic (Electrovalent) Bond
The bond formed by complete transfer of one or more valence electrons from one atom (metal) to another (non-metal), resulting in electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions.
Example - NaCl Formation: Na (2,8,1) → Na⁺ (2,8) + e⁻ [Loses 1 electron] Cl (2,8,7) + e⁻ → Cl⁻ (2,8,8) [Gains 1 electron] Na⁺ + Cl⁻ → NaCl (ionic bond)
Conditions for Ionic Bond Formation: • Metal should have low ionization energy • Non-metal should have high electron affinity • Large electronegativity difference (>1.7)
Properties of Ionic Compounds

1. Physical State: Solid crystalline at room temperature • 2. Melting/Boiling Point: High (strong electrostatic forces) • 3. Solubility: Soluble in polar solvents (water), insoluble in non-polar solvents • 4. Electrical Conductivity: Conduct electricity in molten state or aqueous solution (ions are mobile) • 5. Brittleness: Brittle in nature • 6. Non-directional: Ionic bond is non-directional
4.2 Covalent Bond
The bond formed by mutual sharing of electrons between two atoms (usually non-metals) to achieve stable electronic configuration.
Types of Covalent Bonds: • Single bond: One shared pair (H-H, H-Cl) • Double bond: Two shared pairs (O=O, C=O) • Triple bond: Three shared pairs (N≡N, C≡C)
Examples: • H₂: H-H (single bond) • O₂: O=O (double bond) • N₂: N≡N (triple bond) • H₂O: Two O-H single bonds • CO₂: O=C=O (two double bonds)
Properties of Covalent Compounds

1. Physical State: Can be gas, liquid, or solid • 2. Melting/Boiling Point: Low (weak van der Waals forces) • 3. Solubility: Soluble in non-polar solvents, insoluble in water • 4. Electrical Conductivity: Poor conductors (no ions) • 5. Directional: Covalent bonds are directional
4.3 Coordinate (Dative) Covalent Bond
A special type of covalent bond in which both electrons of the shared pair are contributed by one atom (donor) and accepted by another atom (acceptor).
Notation: Represented by an arrow (→) pointing from donor to acceptor.
Examples: • Ammonium ion (NH₄⁺): NH₃ + H⁺ → NH₄⁺ (N donates lone pair) • Hydronium ion (H₃O⁺): H₂O + H⁺ → H₃O⁺ • Sulfur dioxide (SO₂): Contains coordinate bond • Ozone (O₃): Contains coordinate bond
5. Hydrogen Bonding
The attractive force between hydrogen atom covalently bonded to a highly electronegative atom (F, O, N) and another electronegative atom in the same or different molecule.
Conditions: • H must be bonded to F, O, or N • Size of electronegative atom should be small • High electronegativity difference
Types of Hydrogen Bonding
Intermolecular H-bonding: Between different molecules. Example: H₂O, NH₃, HF (increases boiling point)
Intramolecular H-bonding: Within the same molecule. Example: o-nitrophenol (decreases boiling point, called chelation)

content.label: 🌡️ Effect on Properties
content.text: H-bonding increases: • Boiling point (H₂O > H₂S) • Solubility in water • Viscosity • Surface tension
6. Metallic Bonding
The bond formed by the attraction between positively charged metal ions (kernels) and a 'sea' of delocalized valence electrons.
Electron Sea Model: Metal atoms release valence electrons which move freely throughout the metal lattice, creating a 'sea of electrons' that holds positive ions together.
Properties due to Metallic Bonding: • Electrical conductivity (mobile electrons) • Thermal conductivity • Malleability and ductility • Metallic luster • High melting points (except Na, K)
7. Polar and Non-Polar Covalent Bonds
Non-polar covalent bond: Equal sharing of electrons between identical atoms. Example: H₂, Cl₂, O₂ (electronegativity difference = 0)
Polar covalent bond: Unequal sharing of electrons between different atoms. Example: H-Cl, H₂O (electronegativity difference > 0 but < 1.7)
7.1 Dipole Moment (μ)
The product of magnitude of charge (q) and distance (d) between the charges. It measures the polarity of a bond or molecule.

content.name: Formula
content.note: Unit: Debye (D) or Coulomb-meter (C·m). 1 D = 3.33564 × 10⁻³⁰ C·m

content.text: Applications: • μ = 0 → Non-polar molecule (CO₂, CH₄) • μ ≠ 0 → Polar molecule (H₂O, NH₃) • Helps determine molecular geometry • Percentage ionic character can be calculated

content.label: 📊 Examples
content.text: CO₂ (linear): μ = 0 (non-polar) • H₂O (bent): μ = 1.84 D (polar) • BF₃ (trigonal planar): μ = 0 • NH₃ (pyramidal): μ = 1.47 D
8. Resonance

When a molecule or ion cannot be represented by a single Lewis structure but by two or more structures (called resonating or canonical structures), the actual structure is a hybrid of all these structures.
Examples: • Ozone (O₃): Two resonating structures • Carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻): Three resonating structures • Benzene (C₆H₆): Two Kekulé structures • Nitrate ion (NO₃⁻): Three resonating structures

content.label: ⚡ Key Points
content.text: • Resonance increases stability • All C-O bonds in CO₃²⁻ are equal • Actual structure is more stable than any resonating structure • Resonance energy = Energy of hybrid - Energy of most stable resonating structure
9. VSEPR Theory (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion)
The shape of a molecule is determined by the repulsion between electron pairs (bonding and lone pairs) in the valence shell of the central atom.

Postulates: • Electron pairs repel each other • They arrange to minimize repulsion • Order of repulsion: Lone pair-Lone pair > Lone pair-Bond pair > Bond pair-Bond pair • Lone pairs occupy more space than bond pairs
9.1 Molecular Shapes Based on VSEPR
2 electron pairs: Linear (180°) - BeCl₂, CO₂
3 electron pairs: • Trigonal planar (120°) - BF₃ (0 lone pairs) • Bent/V-shaped - SO₂ (1 lone pair)
4 electron pairs: • Tetrahedral (109.5°) - CH₄, NH₄⁺ (0 lone pairs) • Trigonal pyramidal (107°) - NH₃ (1 lone pair) • Bent/V-shaped (104.5°) - H₂O (2 lone pairs)
5 electron pairs: • Trigonal bipyramidal - PCl₅ (0 lone pairs) • See-saw - SF₄ (1 lone pair) • T-shaped - ClF₃ (2 lone pairs) • Linear - XeF₂ (3 lone pairs)
6 electron pairs: • Octahedral - SF₆ (0 lone pairs) • Square pyramidal - BrF₅ (1 lone pair) • Square planar - XeF₄ (2 lone pairs)

content.label: 🎯 Memory Trick
content.text: Count total electron pairs = (Bond pairs + Lone pairs). Arrange to minimize repulsion. Lone pairs cause more repulsion → reduce bond angles.
10. Hybridization
The process of mixing atomic orbitals of nearly equal energy to form new orbitals of equivalent energy and shape is called hybridization.
10.1 Types of Hybridization
sp hybridization: • 1 s + 1 p → 2 sp orbitals • Linear geometry (180°) • Example: BeCl₂, C₂H₂ (acetylene), CO₂
sp² hybridization: • 1 s + 2 p → 3 sp² orbitals • Trigonal planar (120°) • Example: BF₃, C₂H₄ (ethylene), SO₃
sp³ hybridization: • 1 s + 3 p → 4 sp³ orbitals • Tetrahedral (109.5°) • Example: CH₄, NH, H₂O, CCl₄
sp³d hybridization: • 1 s + 3 p + 1 d → 5 sp³d orbitals • Trigonal bipyramidal • Example: PCl₅
sp³d² hybridization: • 1 s + 3 p + 2 d → 6 sp³d² orbitals • Octahedral • Example: SF₆
10.2 Finding Hybridization

content.name: Steric Number Method
content.note: SN=2 → sp • SN=3 → sp² • SN=4 → sp³ • SN=5 → sp³d • SN=6 → sp³d²

content.label: Quick Method
content.text: For CH₄: C has 4 bonds, 0 lone pairs → SN=4 → sp³ • For NH₃: N has 3 bonds, 1 lone pair → SN=4 → sp³ • For H₂O: O has 2 bonds, 2 lone pairs → SN=4 → sp³
11. Important Formulas Summary

content.name: Steric Number
content.formula: SN = \text{Number of atoms bonded} + \text{Number of lone pairs}

content.label: 🎓 Exam Tips
content.text: • Ionic bond: Metal + Non-metal • Covalent bond: Non-metal + Non-metal • H-bonding: H attached to F, O, or N • sp³ with 0 lone pairs = tetrahedral (109.5°) • sp³ with 1 lone pair = pyramidal (107°) • sp³ with 2 lone pairs = bent (104.5°) • Resonance increases stability • Dipole moment = 0 for symmetrical molecules
12. Common Mistakes to Avoid

❌ Confusing bond pairs with lone pairs in VSEPR • ❌ Forgetting that coordinate bond is also covalent • ❌ Assuming all molecules with polar bonds are polar (CO₂ has polar bonds but is non-polar) • ❌ Mixing up hybridization with molecular geometry • ❌ Forgetting lone pairs when determining shape • ❌ Assuming H-bonding exists in all H-containing compounds (only with F, O, N)
